papers.title: Constraints on the origins of hypervelocity stars: velocity distribution, mergers and star-formation history

abstract.text: In recent years surveys have identified several dozen B stars in the Milky Way halo moving faster than the local escape speed. The origin of most of these hypervelocity stars (HVSs) is still poorly constrained. Here we show that the velocity distribution, and in particular the deficiency in >700 km/s HVSs is inconsistent with binary disruptions by the massive black hole (MBH) in the Galactic Centre. This conclusion holds in the full and empty loss cone regime, and for secular instabilities in eccentric disks. Accounting for multiple close encounters between binaries and the MBH, does not qualitatively change the results. Moreover, there is no observed counterpart population in the Galactic Centre that is consistent with the HVSs. The star-formation history could be tuned explain the HVS velocity distribution, but this tuning would produce a mismatch with the observed HVS flight times. Frequent stellar collisions of the binary components due to interactions with the MBH do not significantly impact the velocity distribution in the Galactic halo. Such collisions, however, can leave observable remnants in the Galactic Centre, and potentially explain the origins of G2-like dust clouds.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The velocity distribution of observed hypervelocity stars (HVSs), particularly the absence of stars with velocities >700 km s⁻¹, contradicts predictions from binary disruption by the supermassive black hole (MBH) in the Galactic Centre under both full and empty loss cone regimes, even when accounting for multiple close encounters. This deficiency persists across secular instabilities and eccentric disk models, and no corresponding population of ejected stars is found in the Galactic Centre. While star-formation history tuning can marginally explain the velocity distribution, it fails to match observed HVS flight times; however, binary collisions with the MBH may produce observable remnants like G2-like dust clouds in the Galactic Centre.

source_fields.title: Constraints on the origins of hypervelocity stars: velocity distribution, mergers and star-formation history

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: The observed velocity distribution of Galactic halo hypervelocity stars is inconsistent with predictions from binary disruption scenarios in the Galactic Centre, suggesting these stars likely do not originate there or that the binary population in the central region differs significantly from current models. Discussion: Collisions in the inner spherical model occur in 20–30% of binary cases, reducing hypervelocity star ejection rates by a factor of a few and slightly decreasing the fraction of stars with velocities above 700 km s⁻¹ due to tidal effects, but do not significantly alter the velocity distribution shape—consistent with observations—despite producing rapidly spinning, initially bright merger remnants that evolve into blue stragglers with enhanced central hydrogen and potential abundance anomalies detectable in lithium, beryllium, and boron. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to distinguish between hypervelocity stars ejected from the Galactic Centre and those originating from external sources by leveraging high-precision tangential velocity data from Gaia EDR3, thereby solving the problem of identifying genuine Galactic Centre-origin hypervelocity stars amidst a population of bound or misclassified late-type candidates. Velocity distribution: The velocity distribution of stars ejected from the Galactic Centre via binary disruption depends on the loss cone regime—following a $v^{-3}$ (full loss cone) or $v^{-1.2}$ (empty loss cone) power law—and this is tested against observational data from Brown et al. (2018) and the S-star cluster, which constrains the expected presence of ejected companions. Appendix A Deceleration in the Galactic potential: Ejected stars are decelerated by the Milky Way's nuclear star cluster and Galactic potential, modeled using a Hernquist bulge, Miyamoto-Nagai disk, and NFW dark matter halo with parameters derived from recent mass measurements and integrated over random times up to 500 Myr.

abstract: In recent years surveys have identified several dozen B stars in the Milky Way halo moving faster than the local escape speed.
The origin of most of these hypervelocity stars (HVSs) is still poorly constrained.
title: Constraints on the origins of hypervelocity stars: velocity distribution, mergers and star-formation

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper analyzes velocity distribution of known HVSs but focuses on theoretical constraints, binary disruption mechanisms, and dynamical modeling without presenting new observational data or object-level catalog.

papers.title: Properties and applications of a predicted population of runaway He-sdO/B stars ejected from single degenerate He-donor SNe
papers.author_names: P. Neunteufel H. Preece M. Kruckow S. Geier A. S. Hamers S. Justham Ph. Podsiadlowski

abstract.text: This study builds on previous works, producing the most extensive prediction of the properties of such a hypothetical population to date, taking into account both Chandrasekhar and non-Chandrasekhar mass events. These results are then used to define criteria for membership of this population and characterise putative subpopulations\\\n  This study contains 6x10^6 individual ejection trajectories out of the Galactic plane calculated with the stellar kinematics framework SHyRT, which are analysed with regard to their bulk observational properties. These are then put into context with the only previously identified population member US\\\\,708 and applied to a number of other possible candidate objects.\\\n  We find that two additional previously observed objects possess properties to warrant a designation as candidate objects. Characterisation of these object with respect to the predicted population finds all of them to be extreme in at least one astrometric observable. We find that current observations support a Galactic SN rate on the order of ~3x10^-7/yr to ~2x10^-6/yr, three orders of magnitude below the inferred Galactic SN Ia rate and two orders of magnitude below the formation rate of predicted He-donor progenitors. The number of currently observed population members suggests that the He-donor scenariois not a dominant contributor to the number of observed SNe Ia. However, we find that, even at the low event rate suggested, the majority of possibly detectable population members is still undetected. The extreme nature of current population members suggests that a still larger number of objects has simply evaded detection up to this point, hinting at a higher contribution than is currently supported by observation. - abridged -

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study performs the most comprehensive prediction to date of the kinematic and astrophysical properties of runaway helium-rich stars ejected from single-degenerate Type Ia supernova progenitor systems, using 6 million simulated ejection trajectories via the SHyRT stellar kinematics framework. It identifies two new candidate objects with extreme astrometric properties, finds that higher-mass (>0.7 M☉) ejected companions are overrepresented in observable space, and shows that current observations support a Galactic SN Ia rate ~3–2×10⁻⁶ yr⁻¹—three orders of magnitude lower than inferred from SN Ia rates—suggesting the single-degenerate channel is not dominant, yet a large fraction of predicted runaway stars remain undetected, implying a previously underestimated contribution to Type Ia SNe.

source_fields.title: Properties and applications of a predicted population of runaway He-sdO/B stars ejected from single degenerate He-donor SNe

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The experiment finds that unbound stars prefer prograde ejection trajectories relative to Galactic rotation and originate predominantly from Earth's immediate vicinity, while bound stars originate from retrograde locations, with kinematic preferences driven by local density and initial ejection conditions in the Galactic disc. Results#2: The fiducial volume analysis shows that within a 2 kpc sphere and two 10° cones extending to 25 kpc, the BII subpopulation accounts for ~10% of the total predicted naked helium star population, while the BI subpopulation makes up ~40% at distances below 15 kpc, declining to ~30% at 25 kpc, with the UI subpopulation being largely undetectable due to its low mass and large distance. Conclusions: The synthetic population of high- and hypervelocity runaway stars and white dwarfs derived from SN Ia channels shows distinct subpopulations with observable properties matching known objects, suggesting a low ejection rate and disfavoring a major role for He-star+WD binaries in producing SNe Ia, though extreme objects remain plausible candidates. Methodology: The paper proposes using the SHyRT code to simulate the stellar kinematics of supernova ejecta in a simplified, static Galactic potential model, tracking their trajectories based on initial positions and ejection velocities drawn from observed spectra to study their dynamical evolution within the Milky Way's gravitational field. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that links observable properties of thermonuclear SNe to their progenitor systems by leveraging spectral and light-curve data to distinguish between single-degenerate and double-degenerate scenarios, thereby addressing the long-standing problem of identifying the true progenitor channels for ordinary SNe Ia. Astrometric properties: The astrometric properties of the stellar population, particularly proper motion, show a strong dependence on distance, with the maximum proper motion increasing significantly as the sample is restricted to closer distances within the fiducial volume. Motivation and context: This section contextualizes thermonuclear supernovae in WD stars by reviewing major accretion channels, emphasizing that while the classical hydrogen donor channel is unlikely to produce high-velocity runaways and contributes little to the SN Ia rate, the focus of the study lies on Chandrasekhar mass explosions driven by helium-enriched accretion, which can occur via sub-Chandrasekhar mass detonations without requiring a hydrogen-rich donor. Time since ejection $< 300$ Myr: In the time since ejection less than 300 Myr, boundness and evolutionary state of runaway white dwarfs are key classifications, with bound objects ("B-type") distinguished from unbound ("U-type") based on Galactic potential dynamics, and evolutionary paths depending on pre-ejection mass, leading to He-stars or proto-WD states. Candidate members of the population: This section identifies US 708 as a rare and extreme candidate member of a population of low-mass helium donors ejected from a single-degenerate SN Ia progenitor system, with its high radial velocity and He-burning status suggesting a mass above 0.3 M☉ and a likely origin in the Galactic disc. Expected contribution to the Galactic SN Ia rate: This section proposes a model-independent estimate of the Galactic thermonuclear SN Ia event rate by using observed, long-lived products of SNe—such as hypervelocity stars—as tracers, accounting for detection biases and population distribution to derive the birth rate of progenitor systems. Qualitative properties of the predicted population: A star qualifies as a member of this population if it is He-enriched, a proto-WD, or a WD, resides in or has recently crossed the disc, has ejection velocities between 400–1150 km s⁻¹, and has a mass less than 1.0 M⊙.

title: Properties and applications of a predicted population of runaway He-sdO/B stars ejected from single degenerate

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper analyzes 6 million simulated ejection trajectories and applies predictions to previously observed candidate objects (including US 708 and two new candidates), comparing simulated properties with real observational data.
